texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Market makers play an important role in providing liquidity to markets by continuously quoting prices at which they are willing to buy and sell, and managing inventory risk. In this paper, we build a multi-agent simulation of a dealer market and demonstrate that it can be used to understand the behavior of a reinforcement learning (RL) based market maker agent. We use the simulator to train an RL-based market maker agent with different competitive scenarios, reward formulations and market price trends (drifts). We show that the reinforcement learning agent is able to learn about its competitor's pricing policy; it also learns to manage inventory by smartly selecting asymmetric prices on the buy and sell sides (skewing), and maintaining a positive (or negative) inventory depending on whether the market price drift is positive (or negative). Finally, we propose and test reward formulations for creating risk averse RL-based market maker agents.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1 Introduction
texts.text: 1 Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Deep reinforcement learning (RL) algorithms have had considerable success in surpassing human level performance in several single and multi-agent learning problems. A key ingredient of these advances has been the availability of gaming and physics simulators which provide a ready stream of experience for developing, training and testing algorithms. However, the financial domain lacks a similar suite of simulators that could be used to conduct research.
texts.text: Deep reinforcement learning (RL) algorithms have had considerable success in surpassing human level performance in several single and multi-agent learning problems. A key ingredient of these advances has been the availability of gaming and physics simulators which provide a ready stream of experience for developing, training and testing algorithms. However, the financial domain lacks a similar suite of simulators that could be used to conduct research.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Simulators provide more than just data for sample-intensive RL algorithms; they also provide a platform to conduct controlled experiments to test what is being "learnt" by an agent, how a policy performs in different scenarios and the causality between changes in the environment and agent behavior. Simulators can also be used to train an agent in a diverse set of scenarios, leading to improved generalization and robustness to changes in the environment. In this paper we build a multi-agent simulator for a dealer market and demonstrate that it can be used to train an RL-based market maker agent and understand its behavior.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Market makers play an important role in providing liquidity to markets by continuously quoting prices at which they are willing to buy and sell. In this paper, we focus on a dealer market (also commonly known as an over-the-counter or OTC market) where there is a single security being traded and market makers continuously stream prices to buyers and sellers (referred to as investors ). The investors, who come to the market to execute their orders, observe these prices and select the market maker they want to trade with. The two types of agents - market makers and investors - interact directly with each other, and only observe the transactions that they are involved in and reference price information from an exchange.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In order to model different competitive scenarios, we formalize the dealer market as a multi-agent system and build a simulator with the ability to instantiate market maker agents with different policies. In particular, we implement an adaptive market maker agent which acts as a realistic benchmark and competitor for the RL agent. We then train an RL-based market maker against different competitors, with varying reward formulations and market environment conditions, and study its behavior and performance.

We then train an RL-based market maker against different competitors, with varying reward formulations and market environment conditions, and study its behavior and performance.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.1 Related Work
texts.text: 1.1 Related Work

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The market making problem has been extensively studied in finance and economics literature, largely as an optimal control problem. Classical models such as Garman (1976), Amihud and Mendelson (1980), Ho and Stoll (1981), and more recently Avellaneda and Stoikov (2008) and Guéant, Lehalle, and Fernandez-Tapia (2013), focus on the role of inventory risk in determining the optimal pricing strategy for a market maker. Other models, such as Glosten and Milgrom (1985), study the role of adverse selection risk arising from informed traders in the market. All these works model a single market maker and make assumptions about the distributions of order arrivals and price to derive analytical solutions for the market maker's pricing policy using stochastic optimal control techniques. In contrast, in our approach the distribution of order arrivals for a market maker naturally arises from the strategies and attributes of other agents in the system.
texts.text: The market making problem has been extensively studied in finance and economics literature, largely as an optimal control problem. Classical models such as Garman (1976), Amihud and Mendelson (1980), Ho and Stoll (1981), and more recently Avellaneda and Stoikov (2008) and Guéant, Lehalle, and Fernandez-Tapia (2013), focus on the role of inventory risk in determining the optimal pricing strategy for a market maker. Other models, such as Glosten and Milgrom (1985), study the role of adverse selection risk arising from informed traders in the market. All these works model a single market maker and make assumptions about the distributions of order arrivals and price to derive analytical solutions for the market maker's pricing policy using stochastic optimal control techniques. In contrast, in our approach the distribution of order arrivals for a market maker naturally arises from the strategies and attributes of other agents in the system.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Market making has also been studied in the agent-based modeling (ABM) literature (e.g. Darley et al. (2000), Das (2005),Das (2008), Jumadinova and Dasgupta (2010), Wah, Wright, and Wellman (2017)), but the agents tend to be simplistic with a view to studying market equilibria and endogeneous price formation rather than developing realistic market making strategies.
texts.text: Market making has also been studied in the agent-based modeling (ABM) literature (e.g. Darley et al. (2000), Das (2005),Das (2008), Jumadinova and Dasgupta (2010), Wah, Wright, and Wellman (2017)), but the agents tend to be simplistic with a view to studying market equilibria and endogeneous price formation rather than developing realistic market making strategies.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Most previous work focuses on market making in limit order book markets where all agents submit their orders to a central matching facility and can observe all outstanding orders and executed transactions in the market. In contrast, there has been relatively little work focused on dealer markets (Guéant (2017), Bank, Ekren, and Muhle-Karbe (2018), Ghoshal and Roberts (2016))where the agents interact directly with each other and only observe the trades they are involved in. Our multi-agent simulation realistically replicates the partial observability conditions of a dealer market allowing us to study the challenges it poses for reinforcement learning algorithms.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.2 Our Contributions
texts.text: 1.2 Our Contributions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We make the following contributions in this paper: (i) we formalize the dealer market as a multi-agent system (section 3); (ii) we define an adaptive market making policy inspired by the mean-variance optimization techniques commonly used in finance (section 4).; (iii) we conduct extensive experiments with competing market maker agents and are able to draw some key insights about what the RL-based market maker is learning. (sections 5, 6). In particular, we show that the RL-based market maker is able to learn about its competitor's pricing strategy and manage inventory by skewing prices; it also learns to exploit any significant market price drift by building inventory; (iv) we propose and test several reward formulations that can be used to create a risk-averse RL-based market maker (section 6.3).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2 Dealer Market - Definitions
texts.text: 2 Dealer Market - Definitions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Prices and Spreads : In a dealer market with a single traded security, each market maker has to stream two prices at each time step - the price at which it is willing to buy ( bid ) and the price at which it is willing to sell ( ask ). We assume that there is also an exchange limit order book market (LOB) for the same security, and that all market makers receive reference pricing information from this exchange.
texts.text: Prices and Spreads : In a dealer market with a single traded security, each market maker has to stream two prices at each time step - the price at which it is willing to buy ( bid ) and the price at which it is willing to sell ( ask ). We assume that there is also an exchange limit order book market (LOB) for the same security, and that all market makers receive reference pricing information from this exchange.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The exchange reference price can be broken down into two components: (a) the mid-price P , which is defined as the average of the best bid and ask in the LOB, and (b) the spread S b ref or S s ref , which is defined as the difference between the price of a buy/sell trade on the exchange and the mid-price. By convention, market makers also quote their buy/sell prices relative to the exchange mid-price i.e. the i -th market maker quotes spreads S b i (buy) and S s i (sell). Note that this implies that the market maker buys at P t -S b i and sells at P t + S s i .
texts.text: The exchange reference price can be broken down into two components: (a) the mid-price P , which is defined as the average of the best bid and ask in the LOB, and (b) the spread S b ref or S s ref , which is defined as the difference between the price of a buy/sell trade on the exchange and the mid-price. By convention, market makers also quote their buy/sell prices relative to the exchange mid-price i.e. the i -th market maker quotes spreads S b i (buy) and S s i (sell). Note that this implies that the market maker buys at P t -S b i and sells at P t + S s i .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Spread PnL : For each trade with an investor, the market maker earns a profit that is referred to as spread PnL . In practice, the spreads are a function of the trade size. Thus, for a trade of size v , the market maker earns a profit of v · S b i ( v ) if it is a buy, and v · S s i ( v ) if it is a sell.
texts.text: Spread PnL : For each trade with an investor, the market maker earns a profit that is referred to as spread PnL . In practice, the spreads are a function of the trade size. Thus, for a trade of size v , the market maker earns a profit of v · S b i ( v ) if it is a buy, and v · S s i ( v ) if it is a sell.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Inventory : As the market maker executes trades with investors, it will typically accumulate an inventory z t , which could be positive or negative depending on whether the agent has bought more than it sold up to time t , or vice versa. By convention, buys are positive and sells are negative in the inventory calculation. 1
texts.text: Inventory : As the market maker executes trades with investors, it will typically accumulate an inventory z t , which could be positive or negative depending on whether the agent has bought more than it sold up to time t , or vice versa. By convention, buys are positive and sells are negative in the inventory calculation. 1

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Inventory PnL : A non-zero inventory exposes the market maker to risk from mid-price fluctuations. If the market maker holds an inventory of z t from t to t +1 , and the mid-price changes from P t to P t +1 , the value of the inventory changes by ( P t +1 -P t ) · z t .
texts.text: Inventory PnL : A non-zero inventory exposes the market maker to risk from mid-price fluctuations. If the market maker holds an inventory of z t from t to t +1 , and the mid-price changes from P t to P t +1 , the value of the inventory changes by ( P t +1 -P t ) · z t .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Hedging : Ideally, a market maker would like to keep a small inventory so as to minimize risk. The market maker could choose to reduce risk by initiating a trade which reduces its inventory (e.g. try to buy if it has negative inventory) - this is referred to as hedging . In doing so, it incurs a cost since it assumes the role of a price-taker in the hedging transaction.
texts.text: Hedging : Ideally, a market maker would like to keep a small inventory so as to minimize risk. The market maker could choose to reduce risk by initiating a trade which reduces its inventory (e.g. try to buy if it has negative inventory) - this is referred to as hedging . In doing so, it incurs a cost since it assumes the role of a price-taker in the hedging transaction.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Internalization and Skewing : The market maker could also try to reduce risk organically by skewing its prices to attract trades that offset its inventory. For example, if it has a positive inventory, it could make its buy prices less attractive and sell prices more attractive to investors. The effect of organic risk mitigation by offsetting investor trades is referred to as internalizaton .
texts.text: Internalization and Skewing : The market maker could also try to reduce risk organically by skewing its prices to attract trades that offset its inventory. For example, if it has a positive inventory, it could make its buy prices less attractive and sell prices more attractive to investors. The effect of organic risk mitigation by offsetting investor trades is referred to as internalizaton .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Market share : As multiple market makers compete in a dealer market, each one captures a portion of the investor trade flow depending on its pricing. The percentage of total trades captured by a market maker, weighted by trade size, is referred to as its market share.
texts.text: Market share : As multiple market makers compete in a dealer market, each one captures a portion of the investor trade flow depending on its pricing. The percentage of total trades captured by a market maker, weighted by trade size, is referred to as its market share.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1 Market Maker Agent
texts.text: 3.1 Market Maker Agent

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The observations, actions and rewards for the i -th market maker at time t are summarized below. The market maker receives observations about investor trades it won in the previous time step and reference prices for current time step. It then decides on the prices to stream for time step t and any hedge trades to execute. Finally, it receives a set of rewards in the form of spread PnL, hedge cost and inventory PnL.
texts.text: The observations, actions and rewards for the i -th market maker at time t are summarized below. The market maker receives observations about investor trades it won in the previous time step and reference prices for current time step. It then decides on the prices to stream for time step t and any hedge trades to execute. Finally, it receives a set of rewards in the form of spread PnL, hedge cost and inventory PnL.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Observations. (i) Trades executed in previous time step: { v i,j,t -1 } j =1: N ; (ii) Inventory z i,t = z i,t -1 + ∑ j =1: N v i,j,t -1 ; (iii) Reference mid-price P t and spread curves S b ref,t ( v ) and S s ref,t ( v ) ; (iv) Market share, or equivalently, total market volume in previous time step.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Actions. (i) Pricing: Determine spreads S b i,t ( v ) and S s i,t ( v ) to stream; (ii) Hedging: Determine fraction x i,t of the inventory z i,t to hedge.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Rewards. (i) Spread PnL: For each investor trade v , the market maker earns | v |· S b i,t ( v ) or | v |· S s i,t ( v ) , depending on whether it is a buy/sell; (ii) Hedge Cost: Market maker pays a cost of | v | · S b ref,t ( v ) if it is selling and | v | · S s ref,t ( v ) if it is buying, where v = -x i,t z i,t ; assuming that the market maker hedges with the exchange; (iii) Inventory PnL: ( P t +1 -P t ) · z t ; (iv) Total PnL =Sspread PnL + Hedge Cost + Inventory PnL.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section, we will define a more realistic, adaptive pricing and hedging policy for the market maker agent. The pricing/hedging algorithms used in this section are inspired by standard meanvariance trade-off formulations used in the mathematical finance literature for pricing and hedging, cf Almgren and Chriss (2001), Bank, Ekren, and Muhle-Karbe (2018), further tailored to suit our framework.

texts.orig: 4.1 Adaptive Pricing Policy
texts.text: 4.1 Adaptive Pricing Policy

texts.orig: The approach we choose for hedging is similar to the mean-variance approach in step 2 of 4.1. In hedging, the market maker has to decide the fraction x of the current inventory z to hedge by initiating an offsetting trade. In doing so, the market maker has to trade off between (a) the cost of hedging (which increases with the size of the hedge trade), and (b) the risk of carrying inventory and being impacted by mid-price changes.

texts.orig: 5 RL-based Market Maker Agent
texts.text: 5 RL-based Market Maker Agent

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We use a standard implementation of the Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) algorithm with clipped objective in Rllib (Schulman et al. (2017), Liang et al. (2017)) to train the RL-based market maker. The input to the policy network includes all the observations available to the agent at that time step (see 3.1), as well as the inventory PnL from the previous time step. The outputs of the policy network are the pricing parameters ϵ b , ϵ s ∈ [ -1 , 1] and the fraction to hedge x ∈ [0 , 1] . By default, the RL agent uses the Total Pnl specified in 3.1 as the scalar reward to train its policy.

texts.text: 5.1 Optimal pricing for spread PnL

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our primary objective is to understand the behavior of an RL-based market maker when it is trained against different competitors. In order to assess whether the RL agent is learning about its competitor's policy, we derive the optimal ϵ b , ϵ s in Theorem 1 for a simplified case where the RL agent competes with exactly one other market maker. (proof in Appendix).

In order to assess whether the RL agent is learning about its competitor's policy, we derive the optimal ϵ b , ϵ s in Theorem 1 for a simplified case where the RL agent competes with exactly one other market maker. (proof in Appendix).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5.2 Reward formulations for risk aversion
texts.text: 5.2 Reward formulations for risk aversion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our basic reward definition of Total PnL models an agent that is not risk averse. However, in practice, a market maker would not prefer its inventory PnL to be volatile, e.g. high losses combined with high gains. To model this risk aversion, we add a penalty term to the Total PnL which aims at reducing the inventory PnL variance. This aversion to mid-price fluctuations is modeled in the adaptive market maker via its risk aversion coefficient. We propose the following risk penalty functions and empirically compare them in our experiments.
texts.text: Our basic reward definition of Total PnL models an agent that is not risk averse. However, in practice, a market maker would not prefer its inventory PnL to be volatile, e.g. high losses combined with high gains. To model this risk aversion, we add a penalty term to the Total PnL which aims at reducing the inventory PnL variance. This aversion to mid-price fluctuations is modeled in the adaptive market maker via its risk aversion coefficient. We propose the following risk penalty functions and empirically compare them in our experiments.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · [InvPnL Stdev] Penalty = -α · Std 10 ( InventoryPnL ) (rolling 10-timestep Stdev).
texts.text: [InvPnL Stdev] Penalty = -α · Std 10 ( InventoryPnL ) (rolling 10-timestep Stdev).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · [InvPnL 2 ] Penalty = -α · InventoryPnL 2
texts.text: [InvPnL 2 ] Penalty = -α · InventoryPnL 2

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · [Asymmetric] Penalty = α · min(0 , InventoryPnL )
texts.text: [Asymmetric] Penalty = α · min(0 , InventoryPnL )

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In our experiments α = 0 . 4 , 0 . 07 and 0 . 3 , respectively, for the three penalty functions. These values were chosen by trial-and-error to be ranges where the RL agent still chooses to trade; a very large penalty term results in the RL agent choosing not to trade at all by setting a high spread.

For each training iteration, we take one step of minibatch SGD via Adam with a minibatch size of 256 and learning rate of 5e-5.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 7 Conclusions and Future Work
texts.text: 7 Conclusions and Future Work

texts.label: text
texts.orig: By using a multi-agent simulator to train an RL-based market maker agent with different competitive scenarios, reward formulations and market price drifts, we have been able to draw key insights into the behavior of the RL agent. We show that the reinforcement learning agent is able to learn about its competitor's pricing policy; it also learns to manage inventory by skewing prices and to maintain a positive (or negative) inventory depending on whether the market price drift is positive (or negative). We have also shown that different reward formulations can be used to create a risk averse RL-based market maker. This allows us to define a spectrum of market makers with different preferences and simulate more realistic competitive scenarios.
texts.text: By using a multi-agent simulator to train an RL-based market maker agent with different competitive scenarios, reward formulations and market price drifts, we have been able to draw key insights into the behavior of the RL agent. We show that the reinforcement learning agent is able to learn about its competitor's pricing policy; it also learns to manage inventory by skewing prices and to maintain a positive (or negative) inventory depending on whether the market price drift is positive (or negative). We have also shown that different reward formulations can be used to create a risk averse RL-based market maker. This allows us to define a spectrum of market makers with different preferences and simulate more realistic competitive scenarios.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An interesting future direction of research, would be to study the effect of adverse selection and informed investors on market makers. Our multi-agent framework is particularly suitable for this because it explicitly models investor agents and allows market makers to counter adverse selection by tailoring their policies to the agent they are interacting with.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In order to illustrate the ability of the RL agent to outperform its adaptive counterpart in specific situations, we study the case where hedge cost is zero (i.e. it doesn't cost anything to hedge inventory), S ref is high so that one is incentivized to trade with investors, but on the other hand, investors are toxic in the sense that they have perfect knowledge of the mid price move on future timesteps, meaning that inventory PnL will always be negative. This setting illustrates the case where e.g. hedge funds possess complex statistical models that allow them to predict accurately future price moves. The adaptive agent is set to 50% target market share and risk aversion of 2.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The adaptive agent currently doesn't keep track of toxicity (i.e. the ability of an investor to predict mid-price move), so it is expected of the RL agent to exploit that weakness. We display in figure 12 the hedged inventory of both agents as a function of timesteps, as well as the total rewards. It is seen that the RL agent indeed learns to hedge significantly more than the adaptive agent in this setting, so that it is able to outperform it.

texts.orig: 7.5 Adaptive agent skewing
texts.text: 7.5 Adaptive agent skewing

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We illustrate in figures 13, 14 the pricing mechanism of the adaptive agent for risk aversions 0, 2. In this setting, the adaptive agent has a target market share of 25% and competes against a persistent agent quoting ϵ s = 0 , ϵ b = -0 . 5 . When the adaptive agent quotes ϵ s = ϵ b = 0 , he would then in average achieve 25% market share (remember that when 2 agents quote the same price, they have probability 50% to be selected by the investor). Hence, in the adaptive pricing policy, if there was no step 2 but only step 1 targeting market share, ϵ s = ϵ b = 0 would be the optimal level, and this is why in figures 13, 14, max( ϵ b , ϵ s ) = 0 , i.e. at every timestep, the maximum of the blue and green lines correspond to step 1 of the adaptive pricing policy.
texts.text: We illustrate in figures 13, 14 the pricing mechanism of the adaptive agent for risk aversions 0, 2. In this setting, the adaptive agent has a target market share of 25% and competes against a persistent agent quoting ϵ s = 0 , ϵ b = -0 . 5 . When the adaptive agent quotes ϵ s = ϵ b = 0 , he would then in average achieve 25% market share (remember that when 2 agents quote the same price, they have probability 50% to be selected by the investor). texts.orig: Table 6 demonstrates that our adaptive hedging policy outperforms alternative policies - persistent, random and a similar adaptive policy which ignores internalization (i.e. v ϵ = 0 ). The test was conducted by evaluating the performance of an agent with an adaptive pricing policy but different hedging policies, against a persistent agent ( ϵ b = ϵ s = 0 , x = 0 . 2 ). As expected, ignoring the effect of internalization leads to over-hedging and poor performance especially when the risk aversion is high (hedge cost of -43 for γ = 2) .

texts.orig: As shown in tables 4 and 5, the adaptive agent is able to outperform its competitors in terms of Total PnL; in most cases it has an advantage on both spread PnL and hedge cost. However, increasing the risk aversion decreases the competitiveness of the adaptive agent because it tends to hedge more and adjust pricing to maximize internalization rather than spread PnL (in Step 2 outlined in 4.1).
